creator: jgopensource@proton.me
text:
Let's summarize the symptoms:
  1. The system fails to boot with zio_read errors
  2. gptzfsboot is used
  3. The disk mfid0 is 11TB in size
  4. When booting from a USB stick everything in the zpool is visible
  5. The system booted successfully for some time before it stopped being able to boot

gptzfsboot uses BIOS interfaces to access the disk. Some BIOS implementations (and even some UEFI ones) can't access disk sectors beyond the 2TB boundary.

When ZFS boot environments are used, creating a new boot environment may allocate sectors beyond the 2TB boundary. If that happens, the new boot environment will be partially or completely inaccessible to gptzfsboot.

The reported problem is explained by the large disk size and limitations of the firmware (the BIOS or perhaps the disk controller's firmware).

Just like gptzfsboot, the BIOS loader also relies on BIOS interfaces so the same problem can occur except the boot would fail at the loader stage, not the gptzfsboot stage.

A good workaround is to create a smaller partition (all sectors below 2TB) which will contain a zpool for booting purposes. A second zpool will contain the rest of the data.

The ugly thing about this problem is that your system will boot successfully many times until one day it suddenly won't boot any more.

In order to prevent this problem from occurring in the future, I have the following recommendations:
  1. The risk of being unable to boot with disks larger than 2TB should be well documented.
  2. The installer should warn you if your ZFS root partition is partially or completely above the 2TB boundary.
  3. The error messages should be improved to at least include the sector number which ZFS was trying to access. Even better if "above 2TB" is mentioned. The best option is to detect that all the failures were above 2TB and print a long informative message.
  4. Some people may already have this unfortunate configuration and we should warn them before the day their systems stop booting. The loader could be modified to detect the problem (e.g. by trying to read the GPT copy at the end of the disk and verifying its checksum) and some script later in the boot process could warn the user.
